Claude.notes: Course intro is strong and the DeepMind mammography case gives a solid opening for CNN architecture. Concept sections are technically pitched at genuine Advanced level. However, insight is generally told rather than discovered by the learner, and Lesson 4 (Implementation & Ethics) has clearly template-generated quiz stems ('What is the primary focus of Implementation & Ethics?') that break the level otherwise established. The Module Test question stems match the same generic pattern.
Claude.topIssue: The 'lab' for every lesson is a chat window with a scenario prompt — there is no actual hands-on execution the learner can perform, only a conversation with an AI persona.

Gemini.notes: CNN, ResNet, skip-connection, and multi-modal transformer explanations are technically sound at the depth an Advanced audience needs. FDA regulatory content (Class I/II/III, 510(k), PMA) is correct. The main concern is uncritical framing of IBM Watson for Oncology as a live 96%-accurate lung-cancer-staging tool — Watson's clinical performance was heavily contested and the product was largely wound down.
Gemini.topIssue: 'IBM's Watson for Oncology began analyzing CT scans with 96% accuracy in lung cancer staging' overstates Watson's clinical validation record and presents a largely retired commercial product as current state of the art.

Perplexity.notes: Case studies cluster in 2019–2020 (DeepMind mammography 2020, CheXNet, Watson for Oncology) in a 2026-dated course. Nothing from the current wave of foundation-model medical imaging (Med-PaLM 2/3, GPT-4V clinical reads, new FDA-cleared radiology AI models from 2024–2025) is present. A learner reading this today would leave with a 2020-vintage picture of medical imaging AI.
Perplexity.topIssue: All medical-imaging AI examples are from 2017–2020; nothing reflects the 2024–2026 foundation-model shift or the current FDA-cleared AI device landscape (now over 1,000 approvals).

Claude.notes: The Atomwise/COVID and AlphaFold openings do set up the concept sections that follow (traditional pipeline crisis → AI-enabled acceleration). Graph neural networks, VAEs, GANs, RL for molecular optimization, and SMILES are introduced correctly at Advanced depth. However, quizzes rely heavily on recall of specific numbers ('94% accuracy', '1.3 billion molecules', 'Up to 60%') rather than reasoning about tradeoffs.
Claude.topIssue: Quiz items across all four lessons are dominated by 'which specific percentage/count did the lesson say?' recall — a learner who understood the ideas but not the exact figures would fail.

Gemini.notes: Halicin (Stokes et al., 2020) and Exscientia's DSP-1181 are correctly cited historic milestones. However, several performance claims read tighter than the underlying literature supports: 'DSP-1181 completing the discovery phase in just 12 months versus the typical 4–5 years' compresses a claim that Exscientia themselves have been careful about, and 'AI-enhanced virtual screening now achieves hit rates of 15–30% compared to 1–3%' presents a best-case number as a general benchmark. Retrospective vs. prospective validation caveats in Lesson 2 are, in contrast, well done.
Gemini.topIssue: Performance figures are frequently quoted as if they were established benchmarks (hit rates, cost savings, speedups) when they come from single vendor case studies that don't generalize.

Perplexity.notes: Every anchor example is 2020: Atomwise COVID screen, Halicin, AlphaFold, Exscientia DSP-1181. In a 2026-dated course this omits the last five years of drug discovery AI: AlphaFold3, Isomorphic Labs, Insilico Medicine's ISM001-055 clinical readouts, Recursion's phase 2 program updates, and NVIDIA BioNeMo — all of which have redefined what 'AI-designed drug' actually means today.
Perplexity.topIssue: Course is anchored on 2020 milestones (Atomwise, Halicin, AlphaFold, DSP-1181); no mention of AlphaFold3, Isomorphic Labs, Insilico's clinical readouts, or BioNeMo — the actual state of the field in 2026.

Claude.notes: Story-to-concept coupling is the tightest in the course: the Replika/Stanford opening genuinely creates the problem that the therapeutic-AI design section then answers. CBT/DBT/ACT/RLHF terminology is applied correctly. The core failure is fabricated-feeling citations: 'Dr. Sarah Chen's study of 2,847 users', 'Dr. Michael Rosen's 892-person UCLA study', 'Woebot's 97.2% crisis detection accuracy across 1,847 intervention events' — these are presented with the specificity of primary sources but are not verifiable.
Claude.topIssue: Multiple case studies are cited with named researchers, precise sample sizes, and specific outcome numbers that do not correspond to identifiable peer-reviewed publications.

Gemini.notes: CBT/DBT/ACT integration and RLHF-on-therapeutic-corpora are named accurately. Woebot's 2017 Stanford RCT is real. But several 'facts' collapse under scrutiny: the EMA does not directly regulate therapeutic AI in the framing used in Lesson 4 (that's the EU AI Act plus MDR), and the '97.2% crisis detection accuracy' figure would be industry-leading if real — the underlying Woebot 2022 Nature Digital Medicine paper does not claim this number.
Gemini.topIssue: Attributes a 'first comprehensive therapeutic AI regulation' to the European Medicines Agency in March 2023; the EMA does not regulate therapeutic AI in this way — the applicable frameworks are the EU AI Act and MDR.

Perplexity.notes: The mental-health AI landscape has moved dramatically in the 12–24 months this course claims to cover. Zero mention of the Character.AI teen-suicide litigation, the 2024–2025 regulatory response to AI companion harms, state-level bans on AI therapist marketing, or the AI-boyfriend/girlfriend wave that reshaped the Replika conversation. The case studies stop at 2023.
Perplexity.topIssue: No coverage of the 2024–2025 wave of AI companion harm cases (Character.AI litigation, state AI-therapist regulations) that fundamentally reshaped this space since the module was written.

Claude.notes: This is the strongest module for narrative→concept coupling of a mechanical framework (Project Nightingale → HIPAA structure → BAA specifics). Covered-entity/business-associate distinction, Safe Harbor's 18 identifiers, and Expert Determination are accurate. Dynamic consent vs. broad vs. tiered consent is well-scaffolded. Weakness is the same course-wide lab problem plus reliance on 2019–2021 case anchors.
Claude.topIssue: Every lab is a chat with an 'AI Compliance Assistant' — the learner never actually drafts a BAA clause, reviews a de-identification checklist, or executes anything.

Gemini.notes: HIPAA technical content is correct: covered-entity/BA/subcontractor tier, Safe Harbor requiring removal of 18 identifiers, ZIP-code cutoff at 20,000 residents, Expert Determination method. Project Nightingale, DeepMind/Royal Free/ICO ruling, and Roche's $1.9B Flatiron acquisition are all accurately described. Roche/Flatiron figures and rationale match public disclosures.
Gemini.topIssue: Doesn't address the well-known 're-identification risk in de-identified datasets' problem — Sweeney's k-anonymity work and modern re-identification attacks on Safe Harbor data would strengthen Lesson 1 significantly.

Perplexity.notes: HIPAA is stable law so most of the module ages well, but the AI-specific privacy landscape has evolved substantially since these case studies. No mention of the 2024 OCR HIPAA-and-AI guidance, HTI-1 transparency rule for AI-informed clinical decision support, state-level health privacy laws (Washington's My Health My Data Act), or the EDPB's stance on health-LLM training data. In 2026, the interesting questions are less about BAAs than about model-training-data provenance and inference-time PHI leakage.
Perplexity.topIssue: Framework is 2020-era HIPAA-plus-BAAs; the 2024–2026 debate is model training data provenance, inference-time PHI leakage, and rules like Washington's My Health My Data Act — none of which appear.

Claude.notes: The strongest module in the course. Obermeyer opening is precisely calibrated: the 17.7% → 46.5% figure lets the learner feel the size of the bias before the concept section explains proxy variables. The 'which is LEAST effective' quiz item (removing race from the dataset) genuinely tests judgment rather than recall, and the pushback on 'removing race eliminates bias' is exactly the misconception the field needs to counter. Rural Mississippi Magnolia case is a real-feeling scenario that maps cleanly to the digital-divide concept.
Claude.topIssue: Lab activity is still just a chat with an 'AI Ethics Consultant' — the sepsis-algorithm audit scenario is a strong setup that deserves a real worksheet, sample data, or fairness-metric calculation rather than a conversation.

Gemini.notes: Technically the tightest module: Obermeyer et al. Science 2019 is described accurately (proxy variable = healthcare cost, protected class = race, remediation shifts identification from 17.7% to 46.5%). Pulse-oximetry racial bias is real (Sjoding et al. NEJM 2020 documented threefold higher occult hypoxemia in Black patients). Fairness metrics (demographic parity, equalized odds, calibration) are named and distinguished correctly. Adversarial debiasing, post-processing threshold optimization, and fairness-aware ML are correctly attributed as separate classes of interventions.
Gemini.topIssue: Under-explores the trade-off between the three fairness metrics — a learner will finish thinking they can all be satisfied simultaneously, when the Chouldechova/Kleinberg impossibility results show they generally cannot.

Perplexity.notes: The Obermeyer 2019 study and pulse-oximeter 2020 findings are still the reference cases for algorithmic bias in health AI, so the core content ages reasonably well. But the 2022 rural-hospital study is now four years old, the FCC 21%-rural-broadband figure has been contested/updated, and there's no mention of the CMS 2024 rule prohibiting discrimination in AI-informed decisions in federally funded health programs, or ONC's HTI-1 transparency requirements for AI-informed clinical decision support.
Perplexity.topIssue: Missing the CMS 2024 non-discrimination rule and ONC HTI-1 transparency requirements for AI-informed CDS — the two most consequential 2024 policy shifts on medical AI equity.

modules.module: M6: Regulation & Safety in Medical AI

Claude.notes: SaMD, De Novo, 510(k), PMA, ISO 14971, ISO 13485, and the 'locked algorithm' requirement are all named and used correctly. IDx-DR's 2018 De Novo approval as the anchor case is exactly right for this module. Google's diabetic-retinopathy-in-Thailand real-world-degradation story is a strong 'validation doesn't equal deployment' teaching moment.
Claude.topIssue: Predetermined Change Control Plans are mentioned but never actually explained — this is the single most important 2024 FDA policy shift for AI/ML SaMD and the module walks past it.

Gemini.notes: IDx-DR De Novo pathway, the Google-Thailand Beede et al. 2020 CHI paper on real-world image quality degradation, and the Paige.AI post-market example are all accurately deployed. EU MDR vs. IVDR distinction is correctly drawn. However, 'FDA's Pre-Cert Program, though discontinued' understates — the FDA formally ended Pre-Cert in 2022 concluding it needed statutory authority; the current framework is Predetermined Change Control Plans (PCCPs) issued 2024 which the module mentions only in passing.
Gemini.topIssue: Treats the 2024 FDA PCCP guidance as a footnote when it is the actual current regulatory mechanism for AI/ML SaMD — replacing the Pre-Cert framework the lesson leads with.

Perplexity.notes: For a 2026 course on medical-AI regulation, the omission set is significant: the FDA's now-1000+ authorized AI/ML-enabled devices list, the finalized 2024 PCCP guidance, the AI Bill of Rights, the EU AI Act's actual entry into force for high-risk AI, and the ONC HTI-1 transparency requirements are all absent. IDx-DR (2018) still frames the module.
Perplexity.topIssue: Regulatory landscape is frozen circa 2020–2022; the finalized 2024 FDA PCCP guidance, EU AI Act enforcement dates, ONC HTI-1 transparency rule, and the ~1000-device FDA AI/ML authorization list are all missing.
